text: Hydrogen fuel cells represent a cornerstone of next-generation power sources for the automotive industry, yet their commercial advancement is hindered by the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). To address this, the development of efficient, durable, and cost-effective electrocatalysts is essential. In this work, we present a non-precious dual-metal single-atom catalyst (SAC) derived from metal-organic frameworks, featuring distinct Co-N₄ and Zn-N₄ local structures. The resulting Zn/Co-N-C catalyst demonstrates exceptional ORR activity, characterized by a half-wave potential ($E_{1/2}$) of 0.938 V vs. RHE and remarkable durability, maintaining its performance with a minimal shift ($\Delta E_{1/2} = -8.5$ mV) after 50,000 electrochemical cycles. When integrated into a realistic alkaline fuel cell, the catalyst achieved a record-breaking peak power density of ~1 W cm⁻², the highest reported to date for single-atom catalysts in such systems. We employed *operando* X-ray absorption spectroscopy to resolve the catalytic mechanism, identifying the Co atom within the Co-N₄ architecture as the primary active center. Our findings reveal that an axial oxygenated species binds to this center to form an O$_{ads}$-Co-N₄ moiety during the reaction. Furthermore, potential-dependent microkinetic modeling and core-level shift calculations corroborate our experimental data, offering a deeper look into the oxygen bonding dynamics at the Co-N₄ sites. This study provides a rigorous mechanistic framework for dual-metal SACs and offers a blueprint for the design of high-performance electrocatalysts for renewable energy applications.
